cases.name: Mitral Stenosis
cases.examination_sequence: General Inspection → Pulse → JVP → Precordium: Inspection → Palpation → Auscultation → Lung bases → Peripheral edema

actions: Look for malar flush from the foot of the bed — bilateral pink cheeks sparing the nose.
Note body habitus — cardiac cachexia suggests chronicity.
Check for sternotomy or lateral thoracotomy scars (prior mitral surgery).
Locate apex beat: count down from 2nd ICS at sternal angle. Normal = 5th ICS MCL. In MS, apex is NOT displaced (LV not dilated). Character should be TAPPING (palpable S1).
Left parasternal heave: place heel of palm on left sternal border. Feel for sustained systolic lift (RV hypertrophy).
Palpable P2: feel at left 2nd ICS for a discrete impulse coinciding with S2 (severe pulmonary HTN).
Diastolic thrill at apex: rare but possible in severe MS. Roll patient to left lateral, palpate with palm at apex.
findings_here: Tapping apex at 5th ICS MCL, NOT displaced

actions: Start with diaphragm at aortic area — listen for S2, assess A2-P2 splitting, check for AR murmur (co-existing AR common in RHD).
Pulmonary area with diaphragm — assess loudness of P2. Loud P2 = pulmonary HTN. Listen for Graham Steell murmur (EDM of functional PR from severe pulmonary HTN).
Tricuspid area with diaphragm — listen for TR murmur (functional, from RV dilatation). Check Carvallo sign (increases with inspiration).

CRITICAL STEP — Mitral area: First listen with diaphragm for S1 loudness. Then switch to BELL, position patient in LEFT LATERAL DECUBITUS, and ask patient to exhale and hold. Listen for: (1) Opening snap (brief, high-pitched, after S2), (2) Mid-diastolic murmur (low-pitched rumble), (3) Presystolic accentuation (crescendo just before S1, from atrial contraction — absent in AF).
Time the murmur against carotid pulse to confirm it is diastolic (between S2 and next S1).
findings_here: MDM with PSA at apex in left lateral with bell
No AR murmur (excludes significant co-existing AR)

skeleton_checklist: Heart sounds (S1 loudness, S2 splitting, added sounds)

presentation.opening: Sir, I have examined this patient. This is a 32-year-old pregnant female, G2P1 at 5 months gestation, who presented with progressive exertional dyspnea of 2 years duration, now NYHA Class III, palpitations for 6 months, and an episode of hemoptysis 2 weeks ago. She has a history of recurrent sore throats in childhood and was diagnosed with a heart problem at age 14 but was non-compliant with follow-up and secondary prophylaxis.
presentation.gpe_findings: On general examination, the patient is conscious, oriented, and has malar flush. She is not in acute distress at rest. Pulse is irregularly irregular, low volume, rate 88 per minute. Blood pressure is 110/80 mmHg. JVP is elevated. There is no pallor, cyanosis, clubbing, or pedal edema.
presentation.system_findings: On cardiovascular examination — Inspection: no scars, no visible apex beat. Palpation: apex beat is tapping in character, located in the 5th intercostal space at the midclavicular line, NOT displaced. There is a left parasternal heave and palpable P2. No thrills. Auscultation: S1 is loud. There is an opening snap followed by a low-pitched mid-diastolic murmur with presystolic accentuation, best heard at the apex in the left lateral decubitus position with the bell of the stethoscope. P2 is loud. No S3 or S4.
presentation.diagnosis_statement: Severe rheumatic mitral stenosis with atrial fibrillation, pulmonary arterial hypertension, and NYHA Class III symptoms, complicated by hemoptysis, in the setting of pregnancy.

diagnosis_layers.functional: Severe, NYHA III, AF, pulmonary HTN, hemoptysis

findings.mechanism: In MS, high LA pressure keeps mitral valve leaflets wide apart at end-diastole (maximally open position). When LV contracts, ventricular pressure rapidly exceeds LA pressure, slamming the widely-separated leaflets shut with force. This forceful closure generates a loud S1. In normal hearts, leaflets are already partially closed by end-diastole, so closure is gentler.
findings.chain: [High LA pressure] → [Valve wide open at end-diastole] → [LV contraction slams valve shut] → [Loud S1]
findings.caveat: Soft S1 = calcified, rigid valve that cannot snap. Indicates advanced MS. Loss of loud S1 means valve is unsuitable for PBMV (balloon valvotomy requires mobile leaflets).

findings.mechanism: During isovolumetric relaxation, LV pressure drops below LA pressure. The stenotic but mobile mitral valve suddenly opens under the high LA pressure gradient, producing a sharp snap sound. The A2-OS interval is inversely proportional to severity: higher LA pressure opens valve earlier → shorter interval.
findings.chain: [LV relaxation] → [LA pressure > LV pressure] → [Mobile stenotic valve snaps open] → [Opening snap]
findings.caveat: Absent OS = calcified, immobile valve (advanced disease). Differentiate OS from split S2: OS is heard at apex, split S2 at pulmonary area. OS does not vary with respiration. A2-OS <80ms = severe MS. A2-OS >110ms = mild MS.

findings.mechanism: Blood flows from LA to LV through the narrowed mitral orifice during diastole. The restricted orifice creates turbulent flow → low-frequency vibrations → low-pitched rumbling murmur. Presystolic accentuation occurs when atrial contraction (in sinus rhythm) produces a final surge of blood through the stenotic valve, increasing turbulence just before S1.
findings.chain: [LA-LV pressure gradient in diastole] → [Turbulent flow through stenotic orifice] → [MDM]. [Atrial contraction] → [Extra flow surge] → [PSA crescendo before S1]
findings.caveat: PSA disappears in AF (no coordinated atrial contraction). EXAM TRAP: examiner may ask 'Your patient has AF — will you hear PSA?' Answer: No. PSA requires sinus rhythm.

findings.mechanism: The tapping character IS the palpable equivalent of loud S1 — the forceful valve closure transmitted to the chest wall. Apex is not displaced because in pure MS, the LV receives reduced filling (obstruction at mitral valve) → LV is normal-sized or even small. Displaced apex would indicate volume overload (MR, AR) which is NOT the hemodynamic problem in MS.
findings.chain: [Stenotic valve restricts LV filling] → [LV not dilated] → [Apex in normal position]. [Forceful valve closure] → [Palpable S1] → [Tapping character]

findings.mechanism: MS → elevated LA pressure → retrograde transmission to pulmonary veins → pulmonary venous HTN → reactive pulmonary arteriolar vasoconstriction → pulmonary arterial HTN → RV must pump against increased afterload → RV hypertrophies → enlarged RV pushes anterior chest wall during systole → palpable heave.
findings.chain: [MS] → [↑LA pressure] → [Pulmonary venous HTN] → [Pulmonary arterial HTN] → [RV hypertrophy] → [Parasternal heave]

findings.mechanism: Chronic low cardiac output + pulmonary HTN → systemic hypoxia → compensatory cutaneous vasodilation in the cheeks (rich anastomotic venous network). CO2 retention from pulmonary congestion may contribute. The butterfly distribution spares the nasal bridge (less vascular).
findings.chain: [Chronic low CO + pulmonary congestion] → [Systemic hypoxia] → [Facial vasodilation] → [Malar flush]
findings.caveat: Malar flush can also be seen in SLE (butterfly rash — but that is erythematous, not cyanotic-pink) and polycythemia. In MS context, it is pathognomonic.

findings.mechanism: Chronic LA dilatation from pressure overload → atrial wall stretch → atrial remodeling (fibrosis, electrical inhomogeneity) → multiple re-entrant circuits → chaotic atrial depolarization at 350-600/min → AV node conducts randomly → ventricles beat at irregular intervals → irregularly irregular pulse.
findings.chain: [LA dilatation from MS] → [Atrial fibrosis/remodeling] → [Multiple re-entry circuits] → [AF] → [Irregularly irregular pulse]
findings.caveat: AF in MS dramatically worsens hemodynamics: loss of atrial kick reduces LV filling by ~25%, and rapid ventricular rate shortens diastole (less time for blood to cross stenotic valve). AF onset often precipitates acute decompensation.

findings.mechanism: Five types in MS: (1) Bronchial vein rupture (MOST COMMON) — pulmonary venous HTN → bronchial veins engorge → rupture → frank hemoptysis. (2) Pulmonary apoplexy — sudden massive rupture from acute LA pressure surge. (3) Pink frothy sputum — acute pulmonary edema (not true hemoptysis). (4) Blood-streaked sputum — chronic bronchial irritation. (5) Pulmonary infarction — from in-situ PA thrombosis or LA thrombus embolism.
findings.chain: [Severe MS] → [↑Pulmonary venous pressure] → [Bronchial vein engorgement] → [Rupture] → [Hemoptysis]
findings.caveat: Hemoptysis in MS signals severe disease. Paradoxically, hemoptysis may decrease as pulmonary HTN progresses (protective arteriolar vasoconstriction reduces capillary bed pressure).

differentials.for: Can produce MDM (tumor obstructs mitral valve), constitutional symptoms, emboli
differentials.against: No tumor plop (low-pitched sound from tumor hitting valve), no positional variation of murmur (myxoma murmur changes with body position), no constitutional symptoms (fever, weight loss, elevated ESR). OS present (myxoma doesn't produce OS). History of RHD clinches the diagnosis.

differentials.for: Produces a mid-diastolic rumble at apex mimicking MS
differentials.against: No peripheral AR signs (no collapsing pulse, no wide pulse pressure, no de Musset/Quincke). No EDM at left sternal border. Opening snap PRESENT (Austin Flint does not produce OS — this is the KEY differentiator). Apex not displaced (in AR, apex would be displaced and hyperdynamic).

differentials.for: Has MS component, may present similarly
differentials.against: Would expect fixed splitting of S2 (from ASD causing equalized atrial pressures throughout respiratory cycle). No fixed split S2 described. ASD also produces a pulmonary flow murmur (ESM at pulmonary area from increased right-sided flow).

differentials.for: Membrane in LA mimics MS hemodynamically
differentials.against: Extremely rare. Typically presents in infancy/childhood with severe pulmonary HTN. No opening snap (no valve involvement). This patient has classical RHD history.

vivas.q: Why is S1 loud in mitral stenosis?
vivas.a: In MS, high LA pressure keeps the mitral valve leaflets maximally open at end-diastole. When LV contracts, the large pressure gradient slams the widely-separated leaflets shut with force, producing a loud S1. This is the palpable equivalent of the 'tapping apex beat.' S1 becomes soft when the valve calcifies and becomes rigid — it can no longer snap shut.

follow_ups.q: When does S1 become soft in MS?
follow_ups.a: When the valve calcifies and becomes rigid. The leaflets can no longer snap shut forcefully. Soft S1 in MS indicates advanced disease with calcification. This also means the opening snap will disappear (OS also requires mobile leaflets). A calcified valve is NOT suitable for PBMV.

follow_ups.q: What is the clinical significance of losing S1 loudness?
follow_ups.a: It indicates progression from a mobile, potentially balloon-treatable valve to a calcified, rigid valve requiring surgical replacement. The Wilkins score component for leaflet mobility directly assesses this.

vivas.q: What is the A2-OS interval and what does it signify?
vivas.a: The A2-OS interval is the time between aortic valve closure (A2) and the opening snap (OS). It is inversely proportional to the severity of MS. In severe MS, LA pressure is very high, so the mitral valve opens earlier after aortic closure (shorter interval, <80ms). In mild MS, LA pressure is lower, valve opens later (longer interval, >110ms).

follow_ups.q: How do you differentiate OS from a split S2?
follow_ups.a: OS is best heard at the APEX (mitral area), while split S2 is heard at the PULMONARY area (left 2nd ICS). OS does NOT vary with respiration, while physiological S2 splitting widens on inspiration. OS has a distinctive high-pitched quality (use diaphragm), described as a brief click.

vivas.q: Will you hear presystolic accentuation in a patient with MS and AF?
vivas.a: No. Presystolic accentuation (PSA) is caused by atrial contraction producing a final surge of blood across the stenotic valve just before S1. In AF, there is no coordinated atrial contraction, so PSA disappears. You will hear only the mid-diastolic murmur without the crescendo ending. This is a classic exam trap.

follow_ups.q: Can PSA ever be heard in AF?
follow_ups.a: There is a rare exception — some authorities describe a 'pseudo-PSA' in AF caused by acceleration of blood flow as the narrowing orifice creates increasing turbulence toward the end of long diastolic pauses. But for exam purposes, the standard answer is: PSA is absent in AF.

vivas.q: What are the mechanisms of hemoptysis in mitral stenosis?
vivas.a: Five mechanisms: (1) Bronchial vein rupture — most common, from pulmonary venous HTN causing bronchial vein engorgement and rupture. (2) Pulmonary apoplexy — sudden massive hemorrhage from acute LA pressure surge. (3) Pink frothy sputum from acute pulmonary edema. (4) Blood-streaked sputum from chronic bronchitis due to recurrent congestion. (5) Pulmonary infarction from in-situ PA thrombosis or LA thrombus embolism.

follow_ups.q: Why does hemoptysis sometimes decrease in advanced MS?
follow_ups.a: Paradoxically, as pulmonary arterial HTN becomes severe and fixed, the reactive arteriolar vasoconstriction acts as a 'second stenosis' protecting the pulmonary capillary bed from the full force of LA pressure. This reduces the pressure transmitted to bronchial veins, decreasing hemoptysis. But it comes at the cost of worsening RV failure.

vivas.q: Why is MS the most dangerous valvular lesion in pregnancy?
vivas.a: Pregnancy causes: (1) 30-50% increase in blood volume peaking at 28-32 weeks, (2) 15-20 bpm increase in heart rate (shortens diastole), (3) 30-50% increase in cardiac output. A normal mitral valve dilates to accommodate increased flow. A stenotic, fixed-orifice mitral valve CANNOT dilate — it cannot increase flow proportionate to volume demand. LA pressure rises acutely, causing decompensation (pulmonary edema, AF, hemoptysis). Maternal mortality in untreated severe MS in pregnancy: 5-15%.

follow_ups.q: When during pregnancy is the risk highest?
follow_ups.a: 28-32 weeks (peak hemodynamic stress from maximum blood volume expansion), during labor (pain, anxiety, autotransfusion from uterine contractions adding 300-500ml per contraction), and immediately postpartum (sudden autotransfusion of uterine blood into maternal circulation).

follow_ups.q: How do you manage MS in pregnancy?
follow_ups.a: Rate control (beta-blockers), anticoagulation (trimester-specific: LMWH → warfarin → LMWH near delivery), conservative diuresis, bed rest for severe cases. PBMV can be performed in 2nd trimester with abdominal shielding if medical therapy fails. Avoid aggressive diuresis (reduces placental perfusion).

vivas.q: What is the Wilkins score and why does it matter?
vivas.a: The Wilkins (or Massachusetts General Hospital) score assesses suitability for PBMV by grading 4 valve characteristics on echo, each scored 1-4: (1) Leaflet mobility, (2) Leaflet thickening, (3) Subvalvular thickening, (4) Calcification. Total score ranges 4-16. Score ≤8 = favorable anatomy for PBMV (80-90% success). Score >8 = less favorable, consider surgical options. Score >12 = PBMV contraindicated.

follow_ups.q: What are the other contraindications to PBMV?
follow_ups.a: LA clot (risk of embolization — must do TEE to exclude), significant MR (≥Grade 3 — balloon will worsen it), heavily calcified valve (Wilkins >12), and severe bicommissural calcification.

vivas.q: What is a Graham Steell murmur?
vivas.a: An early diastolic murmur (EDM) at the pulmonary area (left 2nd ICS) caused by functional pulmonary regurgitation from severe pulmonary HTN. The elevated PA pressure dilates the pulmonary valve ring, causing the leaflets to fail to coapt, allowing diastolic regurgitation.

follow_ups.q: How do you differentiate Graham Steell from AR murmur?
follow_ups.a: Both are EDMs. Graham Steell: heard best at pulmonary area, increases with inspiration (right-sided), associated with loud P2 and signs of pulmonary HTN, NO peripheral AR signs. AR murmur: heard best at left sternal border/aortic area, does NOT increase with inspiration, associated with wide pulse pressure, collapsing pulse, and multiple eponymous peripheral signs.

vivas.q: What is the natural history of untreated MS?
vivas.a: 10-20 year asymptomatic period after acute rheumatic fever (progressive commissural fusion). Once symptoms begin, 10-year survival is ~50-60% without intervention. After onset of AF, survival drops to 3-5 years without treatment. After pulmonary HTN develops, survival is 2-3 years. Once right heart failure occurs, survival is <1 year without intervention.

follow_ups.q: What is the valve area at which symptoms typically begin?
follow_ups.a: Normal MVA is 4-6 cm². Symptoms begin at <2.5 cm² (mild exercise intolerance). Significant symptoms at <1.5 cm² (moderate MS). Severe symptoms at rest at <1.0 cm² (severe MS). Critical MS is <0.5 cm².

investigations.expected: Atrial fibrillation (absent P waves, irregularly irregular QRS). If in sinus rhythm: P-mitrale (broad bifid P wave >120ms in lead II — LA enlargement). Right axis deviation, RV hypertrophy pattern (tall R in V1, deep S in V5-6). NO LVH (unlike MR, AR, AS).
investigations.why: Confirms AF, assesses RV strain. Absence of LVH supports MS over MR/AR.

investigations.expected: Straight left heart border (LA appendage enlargement). Double density sign (enlarged LA behind RV). Splayed carina (LA pushes bronchi apart). Kerley B lines (interstitial edema from pulmonary venous HTN). Upper lobe pulmonary venous distension (cephalization). Mitral valve calcification. Normal-sized LV.
investigations.why: LA enlargement pattern + pulmonary congestion WITHOUT LV enlargement is classic MS.

investigations.expected: Mitral valve: thickened leaflets, restricted opening, commissural fusion, hockey-stick appearance of anterior mitral leaflet. MVA by planimetry <1.0 cm² (severe), 1.0-1.5 cm² (moderate). Mean gradient >10 mmHg (severe). LA dilated (>40mm). PA systolic pressure elevated. Wilkins score ≤8 favors PBMV. Check for LA clot (TEE if needed), coexisting MR, other valve involvement.
investigations.why: Confirms diagnosis, grades severity, determines suitability for PBMV vs surgery.

investigations.expected: May show polycythemia (chronic hypoxia compensation)
investigations.why: Baseline before any intervention.

investigations.expected: Baseline INR (will need anticoagulation for AF)
investigations.why: AF in MS mandates anticoagulation (high thromboembolic risk).

investigations.expected: Rule out thyrotoxicosis as cause of AF
investigations.why: New-onset AF always warrants thyroid check, even if MS is obvious cause.

management.medical: Rate control for AF: Beta-blockers (metoprolol) preferred. Digoxin if beta-blockers contraindicated. Target HR 60-80/min. Avoid calcium channel blockers (diltiazem/verapamil) in pregnancy. | Anticoagulation: Pregnancy considerations: LMWH in 1st trimester (warfarin teratogenic weeks 6-12), warfarin in 2nd trimester (safer, better efficacy), switch to LMWH/UFH at 36 weeks before delivery. INR target 2.0-3.0. | Diuretics (conservative): Furosemide at lowest effective dose. Avoid aggressive diuresis in pregnancy (reduced placental perfusion). | Secondary prophylaxis: Benzathine penicillin G 1.2 MU IM every 3-4 weeks. Continue until age 40 (or lifelong if severe valve disease). This patient's non-compliance caused disease progression. | IE prophylaxis: Amoxicillin 2g PO 30-60 min before high-risk dental procedures. Not routine for all procedures.
management.interventional: PBMV (Percutaneous Balloon Mitral Valvotomy): Ideal for this patient IF Wilkins score ≤8, no significant MR (≤Grade 2), no LA clot on TEE. Can be performed in 2nd trimester with abdominal shielding. Success rate 80-90%, mortality <2%. Splits fused commissures, increasing valve area from ~1.0 to ~1.8-2.0 cm².
management.surgical: Open mitral commissurotomy (if PBMV fails or anatomy unfavorable). Mitral valve replacement (mechanical — requires lifelong warfarin; bioprosthetic — avoids warfarin but degenerates in 10-15 years, may need redo in young patient). In pregnancy: surgery carries 5-10% fetal mortality, reserved for refractory cases.
management.this_patient: Stabilize medically through pregnancy (rate control, anticoagulation, conservative diuresis). Plan PBMV in 2nd trimester if Wilkins score favorable and no LA clot. If PBMV not feasible, continue medical management and plan intervention postpartum. Ensure secondary prophylaxis compliance lifelong.
management.follow_up: Serial echo every 3-6 months. Obstetric-cardiology joint follow-up. Plan elective delivery at 37-38 weeks with hemodynamic monitoring. Avoid Valsalva (assisted 2nd stage). Epidural preferred over general anesthesia.

curveballs.q: What about Atrial fibrillation? How would that present?
curveballs.a: Palpitations, irregular pulse, acute worsening of dyspnea Mechanism: LA dilatation → atrial fibrosis → electrical remodeling → re-entrant circuits

curveballs.q: What about Thromboembolism (stroke)? How would that present?
curveballs.a: Acute hemiplegia, limb ischemia, mesenteric ischemia, renal infarction Mechanism: AF + LA stasis + endothelial damage → LA/LAA thrombus → systemic embolization

curveballs.q: What about Pulmonary arterial hypertension? How would that present?
curveballs.a: RV failure signs: elevated JVP, hepatomegaly, edema, TR murmur Mechanism: Passive (from elevated LA pressure) + reactive (arteriolar vasoconstriction) + fixed (vascular remodeling)

history_questions.q: What symptoms would you ask about in a patient with suspected Mitral Stenosis?
history_questions.a: Based on the typical presentation of Mitral Stenosis: Progressive exertional dyspnea (2 years, now NYHA III), palpitations (6 months), hemoptysis (2 weeks ago). Also ask about associated symptoms, red flags, and impact on daily activities.

cases.name: Mitral Regurgitation
cases.examination_sequence: General Inspection → Pulse → JVP → Precordium: Inspection → Palpation → Auscultation → Lung bases → Peripheral edema

actions: Locate apex beat precisely. In MR, apex is DISPLACED laterally and inferiorly (6th ICS, anterior or mid-axillary line). This immediately distinguishes MR from MS.
Character: hyperdynamic/thrusting — large stroke volume (forward SV + regurgitant volume) causes vigorous contraction.
Check for parasternal heave (present if pulmonary HTN has developed, but less common than in MS).
Systolic thrill at apex: present if MR is severe (grade ≥4/6).
S1: typically soft (incompetent valve cannot close properly). If S1 is loud, consider coexisting MS.

Murmur: pansystolic (holosystolic) — starts WITH S1, extends THROUGH to S2, no gap. High-pitched, blowing quality. Best at apex. Radiates to LEFT AXILLA (follows the direction of the regurgitant jet posterolaterally). Use DIAPHRAGM (high-pitched).

S3: listen with bell at apex. In MR, S3 occurs because the large volume of blood stored in the dilated LA during systole rushes into the LV during rapid filling phase, causing sudden LV wall distension.

Check for MDM: a brief mid-diastolic flow murmur may occur in severe MR (large regurgitant volume flooding back creates high-flow across normal-sized mitral orifice). This is NOT MS — no opening snap, and it is a functional murmur.
Check aortic area for AR murmur (multi-valve RHD is common).
findings_here: PSM at apex radiating to left axilla

skeleton_checklist: Heart sounds (S1 loudness, S2 splitting, added sounds)

presentation.opening: Sir, I have examined this patient. This is a 55-year-old male who presented with progressive exertional dyspnea of 3 years duration, now NYHA Class III, orthopnea requiring 3 pillows, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea for 6 months, and fatigue limiting daily activities. He was diagnosed with rheumatic heart disease at age 20, had an episode of infective endocarditis 5 years ago treated with 6 weeks of IV antibiotics, and discontinued secondary prophylaxis at age 30.
presentation.gpe_findings: On general examination, the patient is conscious but appears fatigued. Pulse is irregularly irregular, normal volume, rate 92 per minute. Blood pressure is 120/70 mmHg. JVP is elevated. There is bilateral pitting pedal edema. No pallor, cyanosis, or clubbing.
presentation.system_findings: On cardiovascular examination — Inspection: midline sternotomy scar absent, no visible apex beat. Palpation: apex beat is displaced to the 6th intercostal space in the anterior axillary line, hyperdynamic and thrusting in character. No parasternal heave. No thrills. Auscultation: S1 is soft. There is a pansystolic murmur, high-pitched and blowing, best heard at the apex, radiating to the left axilla. S3 is present. P2 is normal. No opening snap, no diastolic murmur.
presentation.diagnosis_statement: Chronic severe mitral regurgitation of rheumatic etiology with prior infective endocarditis, atrial fibrillation, LV dilatation, and NYHA Class III heart failure — likely approaching surgical threshold.

diagnosis_layers.functional: Severe, NYHA III, LV dilatation, AF

findings.mechanism: In MR, blood regurgitates from LV to LA during systole. This regurgitant volume returns to LV in diastole (added to normal pulmonary venous return), causing chronic LV volume overload. LV adapts via eccentric hypertrophy (sarcomeres added in series → chamber dilatation). Dilated LV pushes apex laterally and inferiorly. The large total stroke volume (forward + regurgitant) causes hyperdynamic contraction.
findings.chain: [MR] → [Regurgitant volume returns to LV each diastole] → [LV volume overload] → [Eccentric hypertrophy + dilatation] → [Displaced, hyperdynamic apex]

findings.finding: Pansystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla
findings.mechanism: Throughout systole, LV pressure exceeds LA pressure (from the moment the mitral valve should close until the end of ejection). Blood regurgitates continuously through the incompetent valve, creating a murmur that starts with S1 and extends to S2 without gaps. The jet is directed posterolaterally (toward the axilla) because the posterior leaflet is typically the one restricted by rheumatic disease.
findings.chain: [LV pressure > LA pressure throughout systole] → [Continuous regurgitation] → [Pansystolic murmur]. [Posterior leaflet restriction] → [Jet directed posterolaterally] → [Axillary radiation]
findings.caveat: In acute MR (chordal rupture, papillary muscle rupture), the murmur may be decrescendo or even absent (LA pressure rises rapidly, equalizing with LV → reduced gradient → shorter/softer murmur). ACUTE MR IS A SURGICAL EMERGENCY.

findings.mechanism: The mitral valve leaflets fail to coapt (close properly) due to rheumatic damage. Incomplete closure → reduced force of closure → diminished S1 amplitude.
findings.chain: [Incompetent valve leaflets] → [Incomplete closure] → [Reduced closure force] → [Soft S1]
findings.caveat: If S1 is loud in a patient with MR murmur, suspect coexisting MS (rheumatic disease often affects both stenosis and regurgitation of the same valve — mixed mitral valve disease).

findings.mechanism: During diastolic rapid filling phase, the large volume of blood stored in the dilated LA during systole rushes into the LV. This sudden, rapid filling causes abrupt distension of the already volume-loaded LV walls, generating the low-pitched S3 sound. S3 in MR specifically indicates volume overload, not necessarily failure.
findings.chain: [Large LA volume during systole] → [Rapid emptying into LV in early diastole] → [Sudden LV wall distension] → [S3]
findings.caveat: S3 is pathological in adults >40 years. In young athletes, S3 can be physiological. In this clinical context, S3 confirms hemodynamically significant MR with volume overload.

findings.mechanism: In MR, the LV ejects blood into TWO low-pressure chambers: the aorta (forward) AND the LA (backward). Ejecting into the low-resistance LA is 'easy work,' so EF remains artificially elevated even as true contractile function declines. An EF of 60% in MR actually represents early LV dysfunction. The surgical threshold is EF ≤60% (not ≤40% as in other conditions).
findings.chain: [MR] → [LV ejects into low-resistance LA] → [EF artificially elevated] → [True dysfunction masked until EF drops to 60%]
findings.caveat: EXAM TRAP: Examiner asks 'EF is 58%, is the LV function normal?' Answer: NO — in MR, EF ≤60% indicates LV dysfunction and is an indication for surgery.

differentials.for: Also a pansystolic murmur, can occur in RHD
differentials.against: TR murmur is loudest at LEFT STERNAL BORDER (not apex), INCREASES with inspiration (Carvallo sign — opposite of MR). TR has prominent cv waves in JVP, pulsatile hepatomegaly. MR radiates to axilla, TR does not.

differentials.for: Also a pansystolic murmur
differentials.against: VSD murmur is loudest at LEFT STERNAL BORDER (3rd-4th ICS), not at apex. Often has a palpable thrill at LSB. Does not radiate to axilla. No history of congenital heart disease.

differentials.for: Dilated LV + MR murmur + heart failure
differentials.against: DCM causes global LV dysfunction → functional MR is secondary. In primary MR (this case), the valve is structurally abnormal (RHD + IE damage). Echo differentiates: primary MR shows leaflet pathology; DCM shows globally hypokinetic LV with dilated mitral annulus.

differentials.for: RHD commonly causes both stenosis and regurgitation
differentials.against: No MDM, no opening snap, S1 is soft (not loud). If both were present, would need to determine dominant lesion for surgical planning. Dominant MR: displaced apex, S3, soft S1. Dominant MS: tapping apex, loud S1, OS.

vivas.q: Why is the EF threshold for surgery ≤60% in MR (not ≤40% like other conditions)?
vivas.a: In MR, the LV ejects into two outlets: the aorta (forward, high resistance) and the LA (backward, low resistance). Ejecting into the low-resistance LA is easy work, so EF remains artificially elevated even as contractile function declines. A 'normal' EF of 65% in MR may actually represent early dysfunction. By the time EF drops to 40%, the LV is severely and often irreversibly damaged. Therefore, the surgical threshold is ≤60% — at this point, true contractility is already significantly impaired.

follow_ups.q: What other parameter is used for surgical timing?
follow_ups.a: LVESD (LV end-systolic dimension) ≥40mm. End-systolic dimension reflects contractile function better than end-diastolic (which is volume-dependent and always dilated in MR). LVESD ≥40mm indicates the LV cannot contract down to a normal size — objective evidence of myocardial dysfunction.

vivas.q: How do you distinguish primary from secondary (functional) MR?
vivas.a: Primary MR: structural valve abnormality (rheumatic thickening, prolapse, flail leaflet, IE vegetation). The valve itself is the problem. Secondary/Functional MR: normal valve leaflets but dilated LV → dilated mitral annulus → leaflets cannot coapt. The ventricle is the problem. Distinguish on echo: primary shows leaflet pathology, secondary shows globally dilated/hypokinetic LV with tethered but structurally normal leaflets.

follow_ups.q: Why does this distinction matter?
follow_ups.a: Primary MR: fix the valve (repair/replace) = curative. Secondary MR: fixing the valve alone doesn't help because the underlying LV dysfunction persists. Treatment of secondary MR is optimization of heart failure therapy (ACE/ARB, beta-blocker, CRT if indicated). Valve surgery in secondary MR has worse outcomes.

vivas.q: What is the Carpentier classification?
vivas.a: Carpentier classifies MR by leaflet motion: Type I: normal leaflet motion (annular dilatation, perforation from IE). Type II: excessive leaflet motion (prolapse, flail from chordal rupture). Type IIIa: restricted motion in diastole (rheumatic — leaflet thickened, commissures fused). Type IIIb: restricted motion in systole (functional — papillary muscle displacement from LV dilatation tethers leaflets). This patient likely has Type IIIa (rheumatic restriction) with possible Type I component (post-IE perforation).

follow_ups.q: Which types are most amenable to repair?
follow_ups.a: Type II (prolapse/flail) has the highest repair success rate (>95%). Type I with annular dilatation can be repaired with annuloplasty ring. Type IIIa (rheumatic) is harder to repair — often requires replacement. Type IIIb is not addressed by valve surgery alone.

vivas.q: What is the difference between acute and chronic MR?
vivas.a: Chronic MR: gradual onset → LA dilates to accommodate regurgitant volume (high compliance) → LA pressure only mildly elevated → pulmonary edema develops slowly → LV dilates (eccentric hypertrophy) → EF preserved initially. Patient tolerates for years. Acute MR (chordal rupture, papillary infarction, IE): sudden onset → LA cannot dilate acutely (low compliance) → LA pressure rises dramatically → flash pulmonary edema → cardiogenic shock. Murmur may be short/soft (rapid LA-LV pressure equalization). SURGICAL EMERGENCY.

follow_ups.q: Why is the murmur sometimes soft in acute MR?
follow_ups.a: In acute MR, the small non-compliant LA cannot absorb the regurgitant volume. LA pressure rises rapidly, approaching LV pressure by late systole. The diminished pressure gradient means less turbulent flow → shorter, softer murmur. This is paradoxically dangerous — severe acute MR with a soft murmur can be missed clinically.

investigations.expected: AF. LVH (tall R in V5-V6, deep S in V1-V2, strain pattern — ST depression, T inversion in lateral leads). Unlike MS, LVH is present because LV is dilated and hypertrophied.
investigations.why: Confirms LV involvement (vs MS where only RVH is seen).

investigations.expected: Cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio >50%). LA enlargement (double density, splayed carina). LV enlargement (apex extends below diaphragm, lateral displacement). Pulmonary congestion (upper lobe diversion, interstitial edema).
investigations.why: Both LV and LA enlarged (vs MS where only LA enlarged).

investigations.expected: Structural: leaflet thickening, restricted motion (rheumatic), healed vegetation (post-IE), flail leaflet if chordal damage. Severity: vena contracta >7mm (severe), regurgitant volume >60ml, regurgitant fraction >50%, EROA >0.40 cm². LV dimensions: LVEDD >65mm, LVESD >40mm = surgical threshold. EF ≤60% = LV dysfunction. Color Doppler: large systolic jet into LA.
investigations.why: Grades severity, assesses LV function, determines surgical timing.

investigations.expected: Better visualization of mitral valve anatomy, vegetations, leaflet pathology. Essential for surgical planning (repair vs replacement).
investigations.why: Determines if repair is feasible (preferred) or replacement needed.

investigations.expected: Elevated (correlates with degree of LV volume overload and heart failure severity)
investigations.why: Objective marker of HF severity, useful for monitoring treatment response.

management.medical: ACE inhibitors (enalapril): Enalapril 5-20mg BD. Titrate to maximum tolerated dose. Monitor creatinine and potassium. | Beta-blockers (carvedilol/metoprolol): Start low, titrate slow. Carvedilol 3.125mg BD → 25mg BD. | Diuretics (furosemide): Furosemide 40-80mg BD. Add spironolactone 25mg for HFrEF. | Anticoagulation (warfarin): INR target 2.0-3.0. Warfarin specifically (NOT dabigatran/rivaroxaban in mechanical valves or rheumatic AF). | Digoxin: 0.125-0.25mg OD. Monitor levels (therapeutic 0.5-0.9 ng/ml). Toxicity risk with renal impairment, hypokalemia.
management.interventional: Not applicable for MR (no balloon procedure available for regurgitation, unlike PBMV for MS). Percutaneous MitraClip increasingly used for high-surgical-risk patients (clips leaflets together to reduce regurgitation).
management.surgical: Mitral valve REPAIR preferred over replacement when anatomy permits (preserves native valve, avoids anticoagulation, better long-term outcomes). If repair not feasible: MVR with bioprosthetic (avoids warfarin, degenerates in 10-15 years) or mechanical (lifelong warfarin but lasts longer). Surgical indications: EF ≤60%, LVESD ≥40mm, symptoms despite optimal medical therapy, new-onset AF, pulmonary HTN.
management.this_patient: Currently decompensated — optimize medical therapy first (uptitrate ACE inhibitor, optimize diuresis, rate control). Once stabilized, assess for surgery. With NYHA III symptoms, AF, and likely LV dysfunction (prior IE damage + 25 years of non-compliance), he likely meets surgical criteria. TEE to determine repair vs replacement.
management.follow_up: Serial echo every 6 months to track LV dimensions and EF. If EF approaches 60% or LVESD approaches 40mm, intervene even if asymptomatic — waiting for symptoms in MR means LV damage may be irreversible.

curveballs.q: What about LV dysfunction (the 'silent killer')? How would that present?
curveballs.a: Gradual fatigue, exercise intolerance. EF may still be 55-60% (appears 'normal' but is actually depressed for MR). Mechanism: Chronic volume overload → eccentric hypertrophy → eventually myocyte death and fibrosis → irreversible LV dysfunction. Masked by artificially preserved EF (ejecting into low-resistance LA).

curveballs.q: What about Atrial fibrillation? How would that present?
curveballs.a: Palpitations, worsening dyspnea, irregular pulse Mechanism: Chronic LA dilatation from volume overload → atrial remodeling → AF

curveballs.q: What about Pulmonary hypertension? How would that present?
curveballs.a: Progressive dyspnea, RV failure signs Mechanism: Chronic elevated LA pressure → pulmonary venous HTN → reactive arteriolar constriction → fixed vascular changes

history_questions.q: What symptoms would you ask about in a patient with suspected Mitral Regurgitation?
history_questions.a: Based on the typical presentation of Mitral Regurgitation: Progressive exertional dyspnea (3 years, NYHA III), orthopnea (3-pillow), PND (6 months), fatigue. Also ask about associated symptoms, red flags, and impact on daily activities.

cases.name: Aortic Stenosis
cases.examination_sequence: General Inspection → Pulse → JVP → Precordium: Inspection → Palpation → Auscultation → Lung bases → Peripheral edema

actions: MUST assess character at the CAROTID artery (not radial — radial dampens the waveform in elderly due to stiff arteries, making slow-rising difficult to detect).
Place index and middle fingers on carotid (one side at a time). Feel for: (1) Rate of rise (slow in AS), (2) Peak (delayed in AS), (3) Volume (low in AS).
The combination 'pulsus parvus et tardus' (small and slow) is pathognomonic for significant AS.
Check blood pressure: narrow pulse pressure (<30 mmHg) supports hemodynamically significant AS.

Apex beat: locate precisely. In AS, apex is NOT displaced (LV hypertrophies concentrically — thick walls, normal cavity size). Character is SUSTAINED/HEAVING (pressure overload — sustained force against fixed obstruction).
Contrast with MR/AR where apex IS displaced (volume overload → dilatation).
Systolic thrill at aortic area (right 2nd ICS): place flat of hand. Palpable thrill = murmur grade ≥4/6 = significant AS.
Thrill may also be palpable in suprasternal notch and over carotids (transmitted vibration).
findings_here: Sustained heaving apex at normal position

actions: Aortic area (RUSB): use diaphragm. Listen for: (1) Ejection click (early systolic, high-pitched — indicates bicuspid valve that is still mobile), (2) ESM (harsh, crescendo-decrescendo, peaks in mid-to-late systole).
Radiation: follow the murmur to the carotids bilaterally. AS murmur radiates to carotids because the jet is directed into the ascending aorta toward the neck vessels.

Severity clues from auscultation: Late-peaking ESM = more severe. Soft/absent A2 = calcified valve = more severe. Paradoxical splitting of S2 = prolonged LV ejection. S4 = diastolic dysfunction from LVH.

GALLAVARDIN PHENOMENON: AS murmur can have TWO components — harsh ESM at aortic area (standard) + musical/high-pitched component at apex (mimics MR). This is NOT two separate lesions. The high-frequency harmonics of the AS murmur are selectively transmitted to the apex.
findings_here: Harsh ESM at aortic area → carotids

skeleton_checklist: Heart sounds (S1 loudness, S2 splitting, added sounds)

presentation.opening: Sir, I have examined this patient. This is a 68-year-old male who presented with exertional syncope — 2 episodes over 3 months — progressive exertional dyspnea of 2 years duration (NYHA II-III), and new-onset exertional angina for 6 months with no known coronary artery disease. A murmur was detected 15 years ago but was never investigated. He has no history of rheumatic fever.
presentation.gpe_findings: On general examination, the patient is conscious and oriented. Pulse is regular, low volume, rate 72 per minute, with a slow-rising character best appreciated at the carotid. Blood pressure is 110/90 mmHg with a narrow pulse pressure of 20 mmHg. JVP is normal. No pallor, cyanosis, clubbing, or edema.
presentation.system_findings: On cardiovascular examination — Palpation: apex beat is in the 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line, NOT displaced, with a sustained heaving character. There is a systolic thrill at the aortic area. No parasternal heave. Auscultation: S1 is normal. There is an ejection click followed by a harsh, crescendo-decrescendo ejection systolic murmur, loudest at the aortic area (right 2nd intercostal space), radiating to both carotids. A2 is soft. S4 is present. No S3, no diastolic murmur.
presentation.diagnosis_statement: Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis (likely degenerative calcific or bicuspid aortic valve) with the classic symptomatic triad of angina, syncope, and dyspnea — indication for urgent aortic valve replacement.

findings.mechanism: The stenotic aortic valve acts as a fixed obstruction. Blood can only exit the LV through the narrowed orifice at a limited rate. This produces a slow rate of rise in the arterial pressure waveform (tardus) and reduced peak amplitude (parvus). The pulse takes longer to reach its peak and the peak is blunted.
findings.chain: [Fixed aortic obstruction] → [Slow, limited ejection through narrow orifice] → [Slow-rising, low-amplitude pulse]
findings.caveat: In elderly patients with stiff, atherosclerotic arteries, the 'slow-rising' character may be masked — stiff arteries transmit the pulse wave faster, making it feel more normal. Always interpret pulse in clinical context. The carotid is more reliable than the radial for this reason.

findings.mechanism: Fixed obstruction limits the volume ejected per beat → systolic pressure cannot rise normally → SBP relatively low. Sympathetic activation and arterial vasoconstriction maintain diastolic pressure → DBP relatively preserved or elevated. Net result: narrow PP (SBP − DBP <30).
findings.chain: [Fixed obstruction] → [Low SBP] + [Maintained DBP from vasoconstriction] → [Narrow pulse pressure]

findings.mechanism: Pressure overload from AS → LV responds with concentric hypertrophy (sarcomeres added in parallel → wall thickening without cavity dilatation). The thickened LV generates sustained, forceful contraction against high afterload. Because the cavity is not dilated, the apex remains in normal position. The 'heaving' character reflects the prolonged systolic contraction against obstruction.
findings.chain: [AS → pressure overload] → [Concentric LVH] → [Thick walls, normal cavity] → [Sustained apex, not displaced]
findings.caveat: If apex IS displaced in AS → indicates decompensation (LV has failed and dilated). This is a late, ominous sign.

findings.mechanism: In bicuspid aortic valve, the two fused leaflets are still mobile in early disease. At the onset of systole, LV pressure rises rapidly and forcefully opens the mobile but abnormal valve, producing a brief high-pitched sound (ejection click) just after S1. The click indicates VALVULAR level obstruction (vs subvalvular or supravalvular where no click occurs).
findings.chain: [Bicuspid valve] → [Mobile but abnormal leaflets] → [Forceful opening at start of systole] → [Ejection click]
findings.caveat: Click DISAPPEARS with progressive calcification (immobile valve cannot snap open). Absence of click in severe AS suggests heavily calcified valve.

findings.mechanism: During exercise, peripheral vasodilation occurs (working muscles need blood). Normally, cardiac output increases to compensate. In AS, the fixed obstruction prevents adequate CO increase. Peripheral vasodilation + fixed CO = drop in systemic BP → cerebral hypoperfusion → syncope. This is an EXERTIONAL phenomenon — not postural or at rest.
findings.chain: [Exercise] → [Peripheral vasodilation] → [Fixed CO from AS] → [BP drop] → [Cerebral hypoperfusion] → [Syncope]
findings.caveat: Exertional syncope in AS is a CLASS I indication for valve replacement. Survival after onset of syncope without surgery: ~3 years.

findings.mechanism: LVH increases myocardial oxygen demand (more muscle mass, higher wall stress). Simultaneously, coronary perfusion is compromised: (1) subendocardial compression from high LV wall stress, (2) reduced diastolic coronary perfusion pressure (low diastolic BP), (3) shortened diastole at higher heart rates. Supply-demand mismatch → angina, even with normal coronary arteries.
findings.chain: [LVH] → [↑O₂ demand + ↓O₂ supply (subendocardial compression + low DBP)] → [Supply-demand mismatch] → [Angina]

differentials.for: ESM, LVH on ECG, syncope, angina
differentials.against: HOCM murmur INCREASES with Valsalva and standing (reduced preload worsens obstruction); AS murmur DECREASES. HOCM pulse is bisferiens (spike-and-dome); AS pulse is slow-rising. HOCM typically presents in younger patients with FH of sudden death. No ejection click in HOCM (obstruction is subvalvular).

differentials.for: ESM at aortic area in elderly patient
differentials.against: Aortic sclerosis = valve thickening WITHOUT significant obstruction. Normal pulse character, normal pulse pressure, no thrill, no symptoms. Murmur is softer and does not radiate as prominently to carotids. A2 is preserved.

differentials.for: ESM, can mimic valvular AS
differentials.against: No ejection click (obstruction below valve level). May have AR (jet hits valve and damages it). Echo differentiates: membrane or fibromuscular ridge below valve.

vivas.q: What are the causes of aortic stenosis at different ages?
vivas.a: <40 years: unicuspid or bicuspid aortic valve (congenital). 40-70 years: bicuspid AV with superimposed calcification (most common cause in this age group). >70 years: degenerative calcific AS of a tricuspid valve (senile calcific AS, most common overall in Western countries). Rheumatic AS: always associated with mitral valve involvement (isolated AS is NOT rheumatic). In India, rheumatic etiology is more common in younger patients.

follow_ups.q: How common is bicuspid aortic valve?
follow_ups.a: 1-2% of the population — the most common congenital cardiac anomaly. Most are asymptomatic for decades. Progressive calcification and stenosis develop 10-20 years earlier than tricuspid valve degeneration. Associated with aortopathy (ascending aortic dilatation, dissection risk).

vivas.q: What is the significance of the symptomatic triad in AS?
vivas.a: Angina → 5-year average survival without surgery. Syncope → 3-year average survival. Dyspnea/heart failure → 2-year average survival. The onset of ANY symptom is an indication for valve replacement. Once symptoms appear, the disease trajectory is rapid and fatal without intervention. Sudden death can occur at any stage once symptoms begin (1-2% per year).

follow_ups.q: Should you replace the valve in asymptomatic severe AS?
follow_ups.a: Controversial but increasingly yes in selected patients. Indications for intervention in asymptomatic severe AS: (1) Very severe AS (peak velocity >5 m/s), (2) Rapid progression (velocity increase >0.3 m/s per year), (3) Abnormal exercise test (symptoms, BP drop, arrhythmia), (4) EF <50% (decompensation), (5) BNP >3x normal. Close surveillance (echo every 6 months) if none of these present.

vivas.q: How does the AS murmur change with severity?
vivas.a: Mild AS: early-peaking ESM, soft, A2 preserved. Moderate AS: mid-peaking ESM, louder, A2 preserved but softer. Severe AS: LATE-peaking ESM (murmur peaks closer to S2), loud with thrill, A2 soft or absent, paradoxical S2 splitting, S4 present. Critical/end-stage AS: murmur becomes SOFTER again (low cardiac output cannot generate turbulence) — this is deceptive. A patient with severe symptoms and a soft murmur = critical low-flow AS.

follow_ups.q: What is low-flow, low-gradient AS?
follow_ups.a: EF <40% + AVA <1.0 cm² + mean gradient <40 mmHg. The low gradient is misleading — it's low because the weak LV can't generate enough flow to push through the valve. Dobutamine stress echo differentiates: true severe AS (gradient increases with dobutamine as flow increases) vs pseudo-severe AS (gradient doesn't increase because valve opens more with increased flow — the valve isn't truly severely stenotic).

vivas.q: What is the Gallavardin phenomenon?
vivas.a: The AS murmur has two frequency components: a low-frequency harsh component heard at the aortic area (the 'typical' AS murmur) and a high-frequency musical component that is selectively transmitted to the apex. At the apex, this high-pitched musical murmur can mimic MR. This is NOT two separate valvular lesions — it is the same AS murmur with frequency-selective transmission. Distinguishing clue: the apex component is still crescendo-decrescendo (AS pattern), not pansystolic (MR pattern). And the pulse character remains slow-rising (confirming AS).

investigations.expected: LVH with strain pattern (Sokolow-Lyon criteria, tall R in aVL >11mm, ST depression + T inversion in lateral leads). Left atrial abnormality. May show LBBB in advanced disease.
investigations.why: Confirms pressure overload. LBBB explains paradoxical S2 splitting.

investigations.expected: Normal heart size or mild cardiomegaly (LV hypertrophied but not dilated until late). Post-stenotic dilatation of ascending aorta. Calcification of aortic valve (may be visible). No pulmonary congestion (until decompensation).
investigations.why: Normal heart size with symptoms + post-stenotic dilatation is classic AS.

investigations.expected: Valve anatomy: bicuspid vs tricuspid, calcification degree. Severity: peak velocity >4 m/s (severe), mean gradient >40 mmHg (severe), AVA <1.0 cm² (severe), <0.6 cm² (critical). LV: concentric hypertrophy, preserved EF initially. Doppler: high-velocity jet across valve.
investigations.why: Gold standard for diagnosis and severity grading. Determines timing of intervention.

investigations.expected: Assess for coexisting CAD (this patient has angina — need to determine if CAD contributes)
investigations.why: All patients >40 years with AS planned for surgery need coronary assessment. If significant CAD: combined AVR + CABG.

investigations.expected: CONTRAINDICATED if symptomatic (risk of cardiac arrest). In asymptomatic AS, may unmask symptoms or abnormal BP response.
investigations.why: NEVER exercise test symptomatic severe AS.

management.medical: No medical therapy prevents progression: Medical therapy is ONLY for: (1) bridging to surgery, (2) patients who refuse/cannot undergo surgery. | Avoid vasodilators: ACE inhibitors use with extreme caution. Avoid nitrates for angina (use beta-blockers instead). Avoid vigorous diuresis. | Maintain sinus rhythm: If AF develops, cardiovert urgently. Rate control alone insufficient.
management.interventional: TAVI (Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation): increasingly used for elderly patients and those at intermediate-to-high surgical risk. Catheter-based delivery of bioprosthetic valve without open-heart surgery. Similar outcomes to SAVR in many populations.
management.surgical: SAVR (Surgical Aortic Valve Replacement): gold standard. Mechanical valve (lifelong warfarin) for younger patients (<60 years). Bioprosthetic (no warfarin, degenerates in 10-20 years) for older patients (>60-65 years). Ross procedure (autograft: patient's own pulmonary valve moved to aortic position) for very young patients.
management.this_patient: URGENT surgical referral. All three symptoms present = high risk of sudden death. AVR (SAVR or TAVI based on surgical risk assessment). Coronary angiography before surgery. If significant CAD: combined AVR + CABG.
management.follow_up: Post-AVR: lifelong follow-up with echo. Mechanical valve: lifelong warfarin (INR 2.0-3.0). Bioprosthetic: annual echo to monitor degeneration. IE prophylaxis for prosthetic valve.

curveballs.q: What about Sudden cardiac death? How would that present?
curveballs.a: Collapse during exercise, ventricular fibrillation Mechanism: Ventricular arrhythmias triggered by LVH, subendocardial ischemia, or acute hemodynamic collapse during exertion

curveballs.q: What about Heart failure? How would that present?
curveballs.a: Dyspnea (the 3rd symptom in the triad). Once HF develops, 2-year survival without surgery ~50%. Mechanism: Prolonged pressure overload → LVH → diastolic dysfunction → eventually systolic dysfunction → decompensation

curveballs.q: What about Infective endocarditis? How would that present?
curveballs.a: Fever, new AR murmur (vegetation preventing valve closure), embolic phenomena Mechanism: Turbulent flow across stenotic valve damages endothelium → platelet-fibrin deposition → bacteremia seeding

history_questions.q: What symptoms would you ask about in a patient with suspected Aortic Stenosis?
history_questions.a: Based on the typical presentation of Aortic Stenosis: Exertional syncope (2 episodes in 3 months), progressive exertional dyspnea (2 years, NYHA II-III), exertional angina (6 months, no known CAD). Also ask about associated symptoms, red flags, and impact on daily activities.

cases.name: Aortic Regurgitation
cases.examination_sequence: General Inspection → Pulse → JVP → Precordium: Inspection → Palpation → Auscultation → Lung bases → Peripheral edema

actions: COLLAPSING PULSE TEST: Lift patient's arm above heart level. Wrap your fingers around the wrist. In AR, you feel a thudding/slapping pulse against your fingers (rapid rise and rapid fall). Compare with the other arm.
Blood pressure: specifically note the DBP. Very low DBP (<50 mmHg) with wide PP (>80 mmHg) is highly suggestive of severe AR.
Corrigan's sign: look at the neck — visible vigorous carotid pulsation (dance of the carotids).
de Musset's sign: observe patient's head from the side — rhythmic bobbing with each heartbeat.
Quincke's sign: gently press on the nail bed with your thumb to partially blanch it. Release and watch for alternating flushing (systole) and blanching (diastole).
Traube's sign: place stethoscope over femoral artery WITHOUT compression. In AR, you hear a sharp 'pistol shot' sound with each systole.

Duroziez's sign: place stethoscope over femoral artery with LIGHT proximal compression. You hear a systolic bruit (forward flow) AND a diastolic bruit (retrograde flow from AR). This is the only sign that directly demonstrates regurgitant flow.
Hill's sign: measure BP at ankle (popliteal) and arm. Difference >20 mmHg = AR. >60 mmHg = severe AR.
findings_here: Corrigan's, de Musset's, Quincke's, Traube's, Duroziez's all positive

actions: Position patient SITTING FORWARD with full expiration held. This brings the aortic root closer to the chest wall and maximizes audibility of the EDM.
Listen at LEFT STERNAL BORDER (not aortic area) at 3rd-4th ICS with the DIAPHRAGM (high-pitched murmur).
Character: high-pitched, blowing, decrescendo (loudest at start of diastole, fading as pressure gradient between aorta and LV equalizes). Duration correlates with severity: longer EDM = more severe.
Also listen at apex for Austin Flint murmur: mid-diastolic rumble caused by AR jet hitting the anterior mitral leaflet. Differentiate from true MS: Austin Flint has NO opening snap, NO loud S1.
Check for coexisting AS (ESM at aortic area) — mixed AS+AR is common in RHD and bicuspid valve disease.
findings_here: EDM at LSB in sitting forward position

skeleton_checklist: Heart sounds (S1 loudness, S2 splitting, added sounds)

presentation.opening: Sir, I have examined this patient. This is a 42-year-old male who presented with progressive exertional dyspnea of 18 months duration (NYHA II-III), awareness of forceful heartbeats — described as palpitations worse when lying on the left side — and nocturnal dyspnea for 3 months. He was diagnosed with acute rheumatic fever at age 16 with polyarthritis and carditis, and has been irregular with secondary prophylaxis.
presentation.gpe_findings: On general examination, the patient is conscious and comfortable at rest. There are visible head bobbing movements synchronous with the pulse — de Musset's sign. Pulse is high volume, collapsing in character with a rate of 78 per minute, regular rhythm. Blood pressure is 160/50 mmHg with a wide pulse pressure of 110 mmHg. JVP is normal. No pallor, cyanosis, or clubbing.
presentation.system_findings: On cardiovascular examination — Palpation: apex beat is displaced to the 6th intercostal space in the anterior axillary line, hyperdynamic and thrusting. No parasternal heave. No thrill. Auscultation: there is a high-pitched, blowing, early diastolic decrescendo murmur, best heard at the left sternal border in the 3rd intercostal space, with the patient sitting forward in end-expiration. S1 is normal. A2 is present. A mid-diastolic rumble is heard at the apex — Austin Flint murmur. Peripheral signs: Corrigan's sign (visible carotid pulsations), Quincke's sign (nail bed capillary pulsation), and Traube's sign (pistol-shot sounds over femoral artery) are all positive.
presentation.diagnosis_statement: Chronic severe aortic regurgitation of rheumatic etiology with LV dilatation and early decompensation (PND onset), with multiple peripheral signs of hyperdynamic circulation.

diagnosis_layers.etiological: Rheumatic / IE / Bicuspid / Aortic root dilatation
diagnosis_layers.functional: Severe, LV dilatation, wide pulse pressure

findings.mechanism: In diastole, blood regurgitates from the aorta back into the LV through the incompetent aortic valve. This diastolic runoff causes rapid fall in arterial pressure. In systole, the LV ejects a very large stroke volume (forward output + regurgitant volume that refilled the LV) causing rapid rise. The combination produces the characteristic rapid rise + rapid fall waveform.
findings.chain: [Large SV in systole → rapid rise] + [Diastolic runoff into LV → rapid fall] → [Collapsing pulse]

findings.finding: Wide pulse pressure (high SBP, low DBP)
findings.mechanism: High SBP: LV ejects a very large total stroke volume (forward + regurgitant volumes combined) → arterial system receives a large bolus → SBP elevated. Low DBP: during diastole, blood runs off into LV through the regurgitant valve → aortic pressure falls rapidly → DBP drops. The wider the PP, the more severe the AR.
findings.chain: [Large SV] → [High SBP]. [Diastolic runoff] → [Low DBP]. [High SBP - Low DBP] → [Wide PP]
findings.caveat: PP >80 mmHg suggests severe AR. DBP <50 mmHg suggests severe AR.

findings.mechanism: The AR jet strikes the anterior leaflet of the mitral valve during diastole, pushing it partially closed. This creates functional narrowing of the mitral orifice → turbulent diastolic flow from LA to LV → mid-diastolic rumble mimicking MS. Crucially, there is NO opening snap (the mitral valve is structurally normal) and S1 is NOT loud.
findings.chain: [AR jet] → [Hits anterior mitral leaflet] → [Partial closure/restriction] → [Functional MS] → [MDM at apex]
findings.caveat: KEY EXAM QUESTION: How to differentiate Austin Flint from true MS? (1) No opening snap, (2) No loud S1, (3) Peripheral AR signs present, (4) EDM at LSB present. If examiner asks and you mention opening snap as the differentiator, full marks.

findings.finding: Acute vs chronic AR — why peripheral signs are absent in acute
findings.mechanism: In chronic AR, the LV gradually dilates over months-years (eccentric hypertrophy), accommodating the regurgitant volume. Total stroke volume is very large → all peripheral signs develop. In acute AR (IE, dissection), the LV is normal-sized and cannot accommodate the sudden regurgitant volume → LVEDP rises dramatically → LA pressure rises → flash pulmonary edema. Because the LV hasn't had time to dilate, total SV is not increased → no wide PP, no bounding pulse, no peripheral signs. The murmur is short (rapid equalization of aortic and LV pressures). Acute AR is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY.
findings.chain: [Acute AR] → [Normal-sized LV] → [Cannot accommodate regurgitant volume] → [↑↑LVEDP] → [Flash pulmonary edema] → [NO peripheral signs]

differentials.for: Also an EDM in diastole
differentials.against: Graham Steell is heard at PULMONARY area (left 2nd ICS), increases with inspiration (right-sided origin). Associated with loud P2, signs of pulmonary HTN, RV failure. NO peripheral AR signs, NO wide pulse pressure. In this patient, multiple peripheral signs confirm AR.

differentials.for: Wide pulse pressure, bounding pulse, hyperdynamic circulation
differentials.against: No EDM (valve is structurally normal). Look for specific signs: thyrotoxicosis (tremor, exophthalmos, goiter), anemia (pallor, koilonychia). Echo would show normal valve.

differentials.for: Can occur together in RHD and bicuspid valve
differentials.against: No ESM heard. Pulse is collapsing, not slow-rising (which would be expected if significant AS coexisted). If both present, need to determine which is dominant — affects surgical approach.

vivas.q: Name the eponymous peripheral signs of AR and their mechanisms.
vivas.a: All peripheral signs result from WIDE PULSE PRESSURE (high SV → high SBP; diastolic runoff → low DBP). (1) de Musset: head bobbing from carotid pulsation. (2) Corrigan: visible carotid 'dance.' (3) Quincke: nail bed capillary pulsation. (4) Traube: pistol-shot sounds over femoral without compression. (5) Duroziez: systolic AND diastolic bruit over femoral with light compression — the ONLY sign directly demonstrating regurgitant flow. (6) Hill: popliteal SBP >20mmHg above brachial. (7) Mueller: uvular pulsation. (8) Lighthouse: alternating facial flushing/pallor. (9) Landolfi: pupillary pulsation. (10) Rosenbach: hepatic pulsation. (11) Gerhardt: splenic pulsation.

follow_ups.q: Which sign is most specific for AR?
follow_ups.a: Duroziez's sign — it is the only sign that directly demonstrates diastolic retrograde flow (the diastolic bruit). Other signs are manifestations of wide PP which can also occur in high-output states. Duroziez's confirms that the wide PP is from valvular regurgitation specifically.

vivas.q: How do you differentiate Austin Flint murmur from true MS?
vivas.a: Austin Flint: NO opening snap (mitral valve is structurally normal), S1 is NOT loud, EDM present at left sternal border (the primary AR murmur), peripheral AR signs present (collapsing pulse, wide PP), apex displaced (LV dilated). True MS: opening snap PRESENT, S1 IS loud, NO EDM at LSB, NO peripheral AR signs, apex NOT displaced (tapping). The OPENING SNAP is the single most reliable differentiator.

vivas.q: Contrast acute and chronic AR.
vivas.a: Chronic: gradual onset, LV dilates (compensates), large total SV, wide PP, all peripheral signs present, long EDM, symptoms develop over years. Acute (IE, dissection, trauma): sudden onset, LV is normal-sized (no time to dilate), LVEDP rises dramatically, LA pressure rises (premature mitral closure on echo), flash pulmonary edema, NARROW or normal PP, NO peripheral signs, short/soft EDM, cardiogenic shock. Acute AR is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY — mortality without surgery is very high.

follow_ups.q: What echo finding is pathognomonic for acute severe AR?
follow_ups.a: Premature closure of the mitral valve — the LV pressure rises so rapidly in diastole (from regurgitant volume filling the non-compliant LV) that it exceeds LA pressure BEFORE atrial systole, slamming the mitral valve shut early. This does not occur in chronic AR because the dilated LV accommodates the volume at lower pressures.

vivas.q: What are the surgical indications for AR?
vivas.a: Class I indications: (1) Symptomatic severe AR (any symptoms — dyspnea, angina, syncope). (2) Asymptomatic with EF ≤50%. (3) Asymptomatic undergoing other cardiac surgery (CABG, ascending aorta). Class IIa: LVEDD ≥70mm or LVESD ≥50mm (progressive dilatation even with preserved EF). (4) Rapid decline in EF or increase in LV dimensions on serial echo.

follow_ups.q: Why is EF threshold 50% in AR (lower than MR's 60%)?
follow_ups.a: Unlike MR (where LV ejects into low-resistance LA, inflating EF), AR involves ejecting the regurgitant volume back into the aorta (high resistance) in systole. The EF is not as artificially elevated as in MR. Therefore, EF ≤50% in AR represents roughly equivalent myocardial dysfunction to EF ≤60% in MR. Both thresholds aim to intervene before irreversible damage.

investigations.expected: LVH with volume overload pattern (tall R waves in V5-V6, deep Q waves in lateral leads — 'LV volume' pattern). ST-T changes. May show sinus rhythm (AF less common in AR than in mitral lesions — LA pressure is not as severely elevated).
investigations.why: Confirms LV involvement. Sinus rhythm is more common in AR than MS/MR.

investigations.expected: Cardiomegaly with LV configuration (apex displaced laterally and inferiorly — 'boot-shaped' heart). Uncoiled, dilated ascending aorta. No pulmonary congestion until late decompensation.
investigations.why: LV enlargement without pulmonary congestion until late = chronic compensated AR.

investigations.expected: Valve: thickened leaflets (rheumatic), poor coaptation, regurgitant jet. Severity: vena contracta >6mm, regurgitant volume >60ml, holodiastolic flow reversal in descending aorta (severe), pressure half-time <200ms (severe). LV: dilated (LVEDD >65mm), hyperdynamic initially, EF begins to decline at 50-55% (already significant dysfunction in AR). LVESD >50mm = surgical threshold.
investigations.why: Grades severity and determines surgical timing.

investigations.expected: Accurate quantification of regurgitant fraction. Useful when echo is suboptimal.
investigations.why: Gold standard for quantifying regurgitation when echo is equivocal.

management.medical: Vasodilators (ACE inhibitors, nifedipine): ACE inhibitors (enalapril, ramipril) or nifedipine. Use in symptomatic patients not yet ready for surgery, or asymptomatic with LV dilatation. | Avoid pure beta-blockers: Exception: if AF develops, rate control is necessary. Use cautiously. | Secondary prophylaxis: Benzathine penicillin G every 3-4 weeks until age 40 or lifelong with severe disease.
management.interventional: No percutaneous option for AR currently (unlike TAVI for AS). Emerging: transcatheter AR repair devices under investigation.
management.surgical: AVR (aortic valve replacement). Indications: (1) Symptomatic severe AR (any symptoms), (2) Asymptomatic with EF ≤50%, (3) LVEDD ≥70mm or LVESD ≥50mm (progressive dilatation). Mechanical vs bioprosthetic same considerations as AS. Valve-sparing root repair in selected patients (Marfan, annuloaortic ectasia).
management.this_patient: PND onset = decompensation beginning. Needs urgent echo for LV dimensions and EF. If EF ≤50% or LVESD ≥50mm → proceed to surgery. If borderline, optimize medical therapy and reassess in 3 months with serial echo. Restart secondary prophylaxis immediately.
management.follow_up: Serial echo every 6 months for LV dimensions. If LVEDD increasing >5mm/year, consider surgery even if asymptomatic. Post-AVR follow-up as per AS.

curveballs.q: What about LV failure? How would that present?
curveballs.a: PND, orthopnea, pulmonary edema Mechanism: Chronic volume overload → eccentric hypertrophy → eventually myocyte dysfunction → EF drops → decompensation

curveballs.q: What about Aortic dissection? How would that present?
curveballs.a: Tearing chest pain radiating to back, acute severe AR, hypotension Mechanism: Chronic aortic root dilatation (especially in Marfan, bicuspid aortopathy) → weakened aortic wall → dissection

curveballs.q: What about Infective endocarditis? How would that present?
curveballs.a: Fever, new or worsening murmur, embolic phenomena Mechanism: Regurgitant jet damages endothelium → nidus for infection

history_questions.q: What symptoms would you ask about in a patient with suspected Aortic Regurgitation?
history_questions.a: Based on the typical presentation of Aortic Regurgitation: Progressive exertional dyspnea (18 months, NYHA II-III), palpitations (forceful heartbeats, worse lying left), PND (3 months). Also ask about associated symptoms, red flags, and impact on daily activities.

cases.name: Infective Endocarditis
cases.examination_sequence: General Inspection → Pulse → JVP → Precordium: Inspection → Palpation → Auscultation → Lung bases → Peripheral edema

actions: Examine ALL nailbeds for splinter hemorrhages (linear, dark red-brown streaks in the distal third of the nailbed). Note: also caused by trauma — look for distribution pattern and clinical context.
Feel fingertip pads for Osler nodes: tender, raised, erythematous lesions. Press gently — patient will report pain. These are IMMUNE complex deposits.
Examine palms for Janeway lesions: non-tender, flat, erythematous macules. These are SEPTIC emboli causing dermal infarction. Painless on pressure.
Clubbing: develops in chronic (weeks-months) IE. Absent in acute IE.
Check for petechiae on hands and forearms.
Eyes: pull down lower eyelid — conjunctival petechiae. Fundoscopy if available: Roth spots (pale-centered retinal hemorrhages from immune complex vasculitis).
Mouth: dental health (source of bacteremia), petechiae on palate.
Abdomen: palpate for splenomegaly (present in 25-60% of subacute IE). Check for renal angle tenderness (renal infarction from emboli).
Neurological: brief screening for focal deficits (stroke from cerebral emboli — occurs in 20-40% of IE).
Urine: dipstick for hematuria (glomerulonephritis from immune complex deposition — immunological criterion).

skeleton_checklist: Heart sounds (S1 loudness, S2 splitting, added sounds)

presentation.opening: Sir, I have examined this patient. This is a 38-year-old male who presented with an insidious fever of 6 weeks duration, progressive fatigue, weight loss of 5 kg, night sweats, and new-onset joint pains. He has a known history of rheumatic heart disease with mitral regurgitation diagnosed at age 22. He underwent a dental extraction 2 months ago without antibiotic prophylaxis.
presentation.gpe_findings: On general examination, the patient is conscious, febrile (temperature 38.4°C), appears unwell and cachectic. Pulse is regular, 96 per minute, normal volume. Blood pressure is 130/70 mmHg. On examining the hands, I note splinter hemorrhages in two nailbeds and a tender, erythematous nodule on the pad of the left index finger — Osler node. There are non-tender, erythematous macules on the right palm — Janeway lesions. Petechiae are present on the conjunctivae. No clubbing. Splenomegaly is palpable.
presentation.system_findings: On cardiovascular examination — apex beat is displaced to the 6th intercostal space in the anterior axillary line, hyperdynamic. There is a pansystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla — consistent with mitral regurgitation — which the patient reports is different from his previously known murmur (new or changed murmur). There is no diastolic murmur.
presentation.diagnosis_statement: Subacute infective endocarditis on a background of rheumatic mitral regurgitation, likely precipitated by dental extraction without prophylaxis, with Duke criteria fulfilled — 2 major (predisposing valve + new/changed murmur likely positive, pending blood cultures and echo) plus minor criteria (fever, vascular phenomena: splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions; immunological phenomena: Osler nodes, conjunctival petechiae).

findings.mechanism: Osler nodes: circulating immune complexes (antigen-antibody) deposit in skin of fingertips/toes → complement activation → local vasculitis → painful, raised, erythematous nodules. More common in SUBACUTE IE (time for immune complex formation). Janeway lesions: septic microemboli from vegetation lodge in dermal arterioles of palms/soles → focal necrosis/infarction → painless, flat, erythematous macules. More common in ACUTE IE (S. aureus, large vegetations).
findings.chain: [Immune complexes] → [Fingertip deposition] → [Vasculitis] → [Painful Osler nodes]. [Septic emboli] → [Dermal infarction] → [Painless Janeway lesions]
findings.caveat: Memory aid: OsLer = Larger, Painful (Immune); JAneway = Flat, PAINless (Septic). Or: Osler = Ouch (painful); Janeway = Painless.

findings.mechanism: Major criteria (2 points each): (1) Blood cultures: typical organism from 2 separate cultures OR persistently positive cultures. (2) Endocardial involvement: echo showing vegetation, abscess, new dehiscence, OR new valvular regurgitation. Minor criteria (1 point each): (1) Predisposing condition or IV drug use. (2) Fever ≥38°C. (3) Vascular phenomena: arterial emboli, septic pulmonary infarcts, mycotic aneurysm, Janeway lesions, conjunctival hemorrhages. (4) Immunological: Osler nodes, Roth spots, glomerulonephritis, positive rheumatoid factor. (5) Microbiological evidence not meeting major criteria. Definite IE: 2 major, OR 1 major + 3 minor, OR 5 minor.
findings.chain: [Predisposing valve + bacteremia] → [Vegetation] → [Continuous seeding] → [Fever + emboli + immune phenomena]

findings.mechanism: Vegetation on the valve causes progressive destruction of leaflet tissue → worsening regurgitation → murmur becomes louder or changes character. New regurgitation through a previously competent valve = new murmur. This is the most specific clinical sign for IE (in conjunction with fever).
findings.chain: [Vegetation] → [Valve destruction] → [Worsening regurgitation] → [New/changed murmur]
findings.caveat: Murmur may be ABSENT in right-sided IE (tricuspid — low-pressure, small vegetations). Up to 15% of IE cases have no murmur at presentation. A new murmur + fever = IE until proven otherwise.

differentials.for: Fever, arthralgia, renal involvement, can have sterile vegetations
differentials.against: Libman-Sacks vegetations are typically asymptomatic (small, located on atrial side of mitral valve). SLE has butterfly rash, photosensitivity, serositis, ANA positivity. Joint involvement in SLE is non-erosive polyarthritis. Blood cultures will be sterile in SLE.

differentials.for: Fever, weight loss, embolic phenomena, raised ESR
differentials.against: Myxoma produces tumor plop (not vegetations on echo), positional murmur variation, no splinter hemorrhages/Osler nodes/Janeway lesions pattern. Blood cultures negative.

differentials.for: Known RHD, fever, arthritis, carditis
differentials.against: RF arthritis is MIGRATORY (large joints), not fixed small joint arthralgias. RF does not cause embolic phenomena (Osler, Janeway, splinters). Jones criteria differ from Duke criteria. ASO/anti-DNase B titers elevated in RF.

vivas.q: Explain the Modified Duke Criteria for diagnosing IE.
vivas.a: MAJOR criteria: (1) Microbiological: typical organism from 2 separate blood cultures (viridans strep, S. bovis, HACEK, S. aureus, enterococci without primary focus) OR persistently positive cultures (≥2 samples >12h apart, or 3/3, or majority of ≥4 samples). (2) Endocardial involvement: echo showing oscillating vegetation, abscess, new partial dehiscence of prosthetic valve, OR new valvular regurgitation. MINOR criteria: (1) Predisposition (valvular disease, IV drug use). (2) Fever ≥38°C. (3) Vascular: emboli, Janeway, mycotic aneurysm, intracranial hemorrhage. (4) Immunological: Osler, Roth spots, GN, RF positive. (5) Micro not meeting major criteria. DEFINITE: 2 major, 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor.

follow_ups.q: This patient — how many criteria does he meet?
follow_ups.a: Major: (1) Predisposing condition + new murmur (pending echo confirmation for vegetation) — likely 1 major (echo). Pending blood cultures for 2nd major. Minor: (1) Predisposing valve, (2) Fever, (3) Vascular: splinter hemorrhages + Janeway lesions, (4) Immunological: Osler nodes + conjunctival petechiae. At minimum 1 major + 4 minor = DEFINITE IE even before blood culture results.

vivas.q: Differentiate Osler nodes from Janeway lesions.
vivas.a: Osler nodes: PAINFUL, tender, raised, erythematous nodules on FINGERTIPS/TOE PADS. Mechanism: IMMUNE COMPLEX deposition → vasculitis. More common in SUBACUTE IE. Janeway lesions: PAINLESS, flat, erythematous macules on PALMS/SOLES. Mechanism: SEPTIC microemboli → dermal infarction. More common in ACUTE IE (S. aureus). Memory: Osler = Ouch (painful, immune); Janeway = Painless (septic). Both are on the Duke criteria — Osler = immunological criterion, Janeway = vascular criterion.

vivas.q: When is surgery indicated in IE?
vivas.a: 4 main indications: (1) Heart failure from valve destruction — MOST COMMON indication, don't delay for completion of antibiotics. (2) Uncontrolled infection: persistent fever/bacteremia >7 days despite appropriate antibiotics, OR abscess formation (paravalvular). (3) Prevention of embolism: vegetation >10mm with 1 embolic event, OR vegetation >15mm even without events. (4) Specific organisms: fungal IE, highly resistant organisms (MRSA with poor response). Surgery = debridement of infected tissue + valve repair (preferred) or replacement.

follow_ups.q: Should you complete antibiotics before surgery?
follow_ups.a: NO — do not delay surgery to complete antibiotic course if there is a clear surgical indication. Continue antibiotics postoperatively to complete the total planned duration (counting from the date of surgery as day 1 if valve cultures are positive, or continuing the pre-operative count if valve cultures are negative).

vivas.q: What organisms cause IE in different clinical settings?
vivas.a: Native valve — subacute: viridans streptococci (most common), Streptococcus bovis (always colonoscopy to rule out colon cancer). Native valve — acute: Staphylococcus aureus (most common in acute, most common overall in developed countries). IV drug use: S. aureus (typically right-sided — tricuspid valve). Prosthetic valve — early (<60 days): coagulase-negative staphylococci, S. aureus. Prosthetic valve — late (>60 days): viridans strep, enterococci. Culture-negative: HACEK group (Haemophilus, Aggregatibacter, Cardiobacterium, Eikenella, Kingella) — slow-growing, need prolonged incubation.

follow_ups.q: Why must you do colonoscopy for S. bovis IE?
follow_ups.a: Streptococcus bovis (now S. gallolyticus) bacteremia/IE has a strong association with colorectal neoplasia (cancer or polyps) — found in up to 25-50% of cases. The organism colonizes the colonic mucosa, particularly near neoplastic lesions (disrupted mucosal barrier allows translocation). Colonoscopy is MANDATORY even if the patient has no GI symptoms.

investigations.test: Blood cultures (3 sets from 3 different sites)
investigations.expected: Viridans streptococci (most common in subacute native valve IE). Positive from ≥2 sets = major Duke criterion.
investigations.why: MUST be taken BEFORE starting antibiotics. 3 sets 30 min apart from different venipuncture sites.
